description: Some truths follow others. Let one follow.

guides.text: We're given two things: $A$ is true, and $A \to B$ is true. Our goal is to reach $B$, with no extra fluff. This is as direct as reasoning gets.
First, bring the [premise|premise] $A$ into the [context|context]. If the world tells you it's true, you may as well write it down.
Now bring in the second [premise|premise]: $A \to B$. This says that whenever $A$ holds, $B$ follows.

You have $A \to B$ at step 2 and $A$ at step 1. The rule `->-elim` — also known as **[modus-ponens|modus ponens]** — says: if you know an [implication|implication] and you also know its left-hand side, you get the right-hand side. Apply it now.

And $B$ is yours. The proof ends here — no [implication|implication] to [discharge|discharge], because the [premise|premises] were given, not assumed for the sake of argument. Just a clean [conclusion|conclusion].

unlocks.name: →-elim
unlocks.description: If $p$ implies $q$, and you have $p$, then you have $q$. The classic syllogism — *modus ponens*.
unlocks.example:
1. (assume P)  →  {P} ⊢ P
2. P → Q provided as premise
3. (->-elim 2 1) → Q
